verify.summary: The core security goal of Q-530 holds: no code path persists the passphrase as plaintext. SettingsArea.handleSave always hashes a non-blank draft, a blank draft keeps the already-stored value (never re-plaintexting it), the input never round-trips the stored hash back into itself, PrivacyLock compares digests (hashing the typed input) with a direct-compare fallback only for legacy plaintext, and the App migration hashes+scrubs legacy plaintext idempotently under an isPassphraseHash guard. The hash helper is byte-identical to the old inline version so existing hashes still compare, and the SHA-256("abc") test vector pins the algorithm/encoding. However, the NEW migration effect (App.tsx:414-421) introduces a stale-closure lost-update regression: it captures the whole `settings` object and, when the async hash resolves, persists `{ ...settings, privacyPassphrase: h }` using that snapshot. Because its dependency is only `settings.privacyPassphrase`, any OTHER settings field changed during the (async crypto.subtle) window is silently reverted by the resolving migration. This is a one-time-per-upgrade correctness regression, not a plaintext-leak (the passphrase still ends up hashed). Secondary notes: the +3 added tests cover only the pure helpers and give no coverage of the migration idempotency/race or the draft/save reconciliation (the fix's highest-risk surface); and enabling the lock with a blank passphrase silently arms nothing (pre-existing, not introduced here).

defects.description: The one-time migration effect captures the entire `settings` object in its closure and, when the async hashPassphrase promise resolves, re-persists `{ ...settings, privacyPassphrase: h }` from that STALE snapshot. Its dependency array is only `[settings.privacyPassphrase]`, so any other settings field mutated (via updateSettings) between the effect firing and the promise resolving is not seen by the closure and gets clobbered/reverted when the migration writes the stale snapshot back to state + localStorage + the WS settings_update frame.
defects.failureScenario: First load after upgrading past Q-530 with a legacy PLAINTEXT passphrase 'pw' and privacyLockEnabled true. Migration effect fires: passphraseMigratedRef=true, hashPassphrase('pw') pending, closure captures S0 with themePreference:'system'. Before the hash resolves, the user (or an auto theme sync) picks Dark mode -> onThemeChange('dark') -> updateSettings({...settings, themePreference:'dark'}); privacyPassphrase is still 'pw', so the migration effect's dep is unchanged and it does NOT re-run. The pending hash then resolves and runs updateSettings({ ...S0, privacyPassphrase: h }), which writes themePreference:'system' back to state and localStorage, silently reverting the user's Dark selection (and re-broadcasting the stale non-passphrase fields to the server). Same clobber applies to any field changed during that window (contextSize, memorySafety, dedupe, lock-enabled). One-time per upgrade, but deterministic for that interleaving.

verify.summary: The core fix is correct and achieves its goal: WS connect now calls activeSessionId() (adopt) instead of startSession() (roll), so a second tab, a page refresh, or a reconnect after a network blip resumes the in-progress session instead of clobbering it — I could not break the reconnect/second-tab adoption path, and the added service-level test is a valid (if narrow) invariant. However, the commit introduces a NEW regression through the mechanism it adds: the unguarded `new_session` WS frame. The server handler (src/websocket/index.ts:443-450) calls startSession() synchronously with no isGenerating/single-flight guard, while the chat path awaits generalChat() at index.ts:297 and only persists the turn afterward at index.ts:335-336 via addMessage() — which uses whatever session is current at completion time (no captured session ref). Because "New Conversation" (App.tsx:543 onNewGoal → resetSession) and the mode-switch (App.tsx:535-540) both send `new_session` unconditionally with no status gating, a click mid-generation rolls a fresh empty session, and the completing turn lands in it instead of the conversation the user was in — the outgoing session is flushed WITHOUT that turn. This is the same class of misplacement the fix set out to eliminate, and it did NOT exist before this commit (pre-fix, resetSession sent nothing to the backend, so the backend kept the same session). I reproduced the ordering at the service level: the completed user+assistant turn lands in the new empty session, not the original. Q-275's single-flight guard (index.ts:271-276) covers concurrent `chat` but not `new_session`, so it does not protect this. The added unit test only checks adopt-vs-roll on the service and gives no coverage of the new WS `new_session` path, so it is false confidence w.r.t. the introduced race.

defects.description: The new_session WS handler calls sessionService.startSession() with no guard against an in-flight chat generation. A chat is persisted only after generalChat() resolves (index.ts:335-336) using the CURRENT session at completion time (no captured session ref). Since 'New Conversation' (App.tsx:543) and the chat/general_chat mode-switch (App.tsx:535-540) both fire resetSession() -> new_session unconditionally with no status==='running' gating, a click/switch mid-generation rolls a fresh empty session and the completing turn lands there instead of the original conversation, which is flushed WITHOUT that turn. This is a newly-introduced regression: pre-fix, resetSession sent nothing to the backend so the session was never rolled by the client.
defects.failureScenario: User is in general_chat, sends 'Hello' (session A becomes current, generalChat streaming). Before it finishes, user clicks 'New Conversation' (or switches to agent/chat view). Server processes new_session: startSession() flushes A and creates empty B, currentSessionId=B. generalChat resolves; index.ts:335-336 addMessage('user','Hello')+addMessage('assistant',reply) append to B. Result: the just-completed turn is stored in the fresh 'empty' conversation B, while session A (which the user was viewing) is persisted without it — the message lands in the wrong session, the same hijack the fix targeted. Fix: guard new_session on !isCurrentlyGenerating (reject or defer like Q-275), and/or gate the New Conversation button / view-switch on status!=='running', and/or capture the session id at chat-start so addMessage writes back to the originating session.

verify.summary: activateSession() itself is correct in isolation (flush-before-switch, unknown-id no-op, already-active no-op all hold), but the WS wiring reintroduces the exact class of bug Q-597 set out to fix. The `activate_session` handler has NO guard against in-flight chat generation, while the `chat` handler defers its addMessage() persistence until AFTER an awaited generalChat(). Because ws.on('message', async ...) callbacks interleave across await points, an activate_session that arrives mid-generation switches currentSession before the pending chat turn is written — so the completed reply lands in the newly-opened conversation instead of the one it was typed in. The commit message explicitly claims to handle "activating while a mission is mid-run," but the fix does not. The added unit test is fully synchronous and never exercises this interleaving, giving false confidence.

defects.description: The `activate_session` handler runs with no guard against an in-flight chat generation. The `chat` handler awaits generalChat() at line 297 and only persists the turn via addMessage() at lines 335-336 AFTER the await resolves. Since ws.on('message', async ...) callbacks interleave across await points, an activate_session dispatched during that await calls activateSession(B), which flushNow()s A and sets currentSession=B (lines 173-175 of session.service.ts) before the pending addMessage() calls run — so those calls append A's user+assistant turn to B.
defects.failureScenario: User is in session A and sends a chat message X. Server enters the chat handler, passes the isGenerating guard, and parks at `await generalChat(...)` (streaming tokens). Mid-stream, the user clicks past conversation B in the sidebar; the client's loadSession(B) sends {type:'activate_session', sessionId:B}. Between token callbacks the event loop dispatches it: activateSession(B) flushes A (WITHOUT X's turn, which isn't persisted yet) and makes B current. Generation finishes; lines 335-336 run addMessage('user','X') / addMessage('assistant', reply) against currentSession=B. Result: A's conversation turn is written into B — the wrong session, the very failure mode Q-597 claims to fix.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED — the defect is real and I could not refute it. The chat handler (src/websocket/index.ts:264-337) awaits generalChat() at line 297 and only persists the turn via addMessage('user', message)/addMessage('assistant', result.text) at lines 335-336 AFTER that await resolves, without ever pinning the session id it started with. Node's ws.on('message', async ...) callbacks genuinely interleave across await points: while one invocation is parked at the await, the event loop dispatches the next 'message' frame and runs its fully-synchronous body. The activate_session branch (lines 451-459) is synchronous and is NOT covered by the single-flight guard at lines 273-276 (that guard only rejects a concurrent 'chat'/'start' when isCurrentlyGenerating is true). activateSession() (src/services/session.service.ts:169-178) then synchronously flushNow()s the outgoing session and sets currentSession=B / currentSessionId=B (lines 173-175) before the parked chat handler resumes. When generalChat resolves, lines 335-336 run addMessage against the now-current session B, writing A's captured user text (line 266) plus the assistant reply into B. Because Q-286 defers persisting the user turn until after the await, A's message X was never written to A and flushNow() saves A without it — so A loses X and B gains A's whole turn: exactly the wrong-session write Q-597 set out to eliminate. The trigger is user-reachable: the client's loadSession (ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:421-441) sends activate_session with no status==='running' guard (unlike sendGoal at line 289), so clicking a Recent conversation mid-stream fires it. The new test in tests/session.service.test.ts exercises activateSession only in the synchronous, non-interleaved path, so it passes while the race survives — false confidence, not a refutation. Attempted refutations (single-flight guard, an ordering guarantee, a session pin captured before the await, a client-side disable during generation) all fail — none exist in the read code.

defects.description: The Q-597 test is entirely synchronous and only drives SessionService.activateSession() in isolation (startSession → addMessage → startSession → activateSession → addMessage). It never exercises the WS chat path, the awaited generalChat(), or an activate_session arriving mid-generation. It therefore passes while the real integration defect (P1 above) is live, giving false confidence that 'a new message lands in A, not B' — a guarantee that does NOT hold once generation is concurrent with the activate.
defects.failureScenario: The suite is green (463 tests) and the commit is merged as fixing 'message landing in the wrong session,' yet the concurrent-generation contamination ships undetected because no test interleaves activate_session with an in-flight chat turn.

verify.summary: The core res.ok logic is correct: handleDeleteNote now gates the optimistic remove on r.ok (Q-529 fixed — a failed DELETE no longer looks successful), refreshCatalog guards r.ok before .json() (Q-527 fixed — a 5xx no longer blanks the catalog), and handleDownloadModel/handleDeleteModel surface !res.ok inline (Q-528). The finally blocks always clear the spinner, so there is no stuck-spinner on the single-action path, and res.json().catch(()=>null) plus the (${status}) fallback degrade gracefully on empty/invalid error bodies. Docs.tsx (fetch → apiFetch) is a pure, safe swap — apiFetch only adds a header, and the guarded !response.ok / text() path is unchanged; SOLID. HOWEVER, the NEW modelActionError state the fix introduces is a single slot that is only cleared inside the handler for the model being acted on. Because the Download/Delete buttons are disabled only per-model (isActing = modelActionId === m.id) and 13 models render at once, a user can start an action on model B while model A's kickoff POST is still in flight. If both fail, whichever resolves LAST overwrites the other's error — so one genuine failure is silently discarded, reproducing the exact 'looked like it worked' bug Q-528 set out to fix, now under concurrency. Separately, the error is never cleared on success or when a model transitions to 'downloaded', so a stale 'Download failed' line can render beneath a now-Ready row after the 2s poll picks up a WS-completed download. The headline fixes are sound; the new error surface is race-fragile and can go stale.

defects.description: modelActionError is a single state slot, but Download/Delete buttons are disabled only per-model (isActing = modelActionId === m.id) and 13 catalog rows render simultaneously. Under two overlapping failed actions, the later-resolving handler's setModelActionError overwrites the earlier one's error, so one genuine failure is silently dropped — reintroducing, under concurrency, the exact 'cleared the spinner as if it worked' bug Q-528 claims to fix.
defects.failureScenario: User clicks Download on model A (kickoff POST in flight due to latency), then clicks Download on model B before A resolves (B's button is enabled — per-model disable only blocks A). Both POSTs return 400. B resolves first → error {id:'B'} shows. Then A resolves → setModelActionError({id:'A'}) at line 192 overwrites B's error. Result: only A shows an error; B's failure is invisible, so the user believes B's download started. Same slot-clobber applies symmetrically to handleDeleteModel line 213.

defects.description: modelActionError is never cleared on a successful action or when the model row transitions to isDownloaded; it is only reset (to null) at the start of the next Download/Delete handler. If no further model action is taken, a stale 'Download failed' error keeps rendering — and can end up beneath a model that has since become 'Ready'.
defects.failureScenario: A download for model A completes asynchronously over the WS model_download_progress channel while a later redundant Download-kickoff POST for A returns 400. handleDownloadModel sets modelActionError={id:'A','Download failed (400)'}. The finally-scheduled refreshCatalog (2s) then reports A as downloaded, so the row renders the green 'Ready' badge + Delete button AND the stale red 'Download failed (400)' line (rendered at line 626 solely on modelActionError?.id === m.id), a self-contradictory surface the user must dismiss by triggering another action.

verify.summary:
d73ed98 removes the dead `classifyWithLlmFallback` (a second, divergent 5-category LLM classifier) from intentClassifier.ts, adds an explanatory comment above INTENT_CLASSIFIER_PROMPT in agent.service.ts, and rewrites the cache-bound test to exercise the single remaining write path (classifyIntent). I adversarially attacked all four named vectors and could not break the fix.

(1) LOST CALLER BEHAVIOR — none. Repo-wide grep for `classifyWithLlmFallback` returns only comment/journal-text references; zero production or test *code* calls it. Its 5-category LLM prompt was never routed anywhere — the live agent path uses only the 2-category chat-vs-action INTENT_CLASSIFIER_PROMPT — so nothing downstream ever consumed an `llm`-sourced math/code/image classification. Clean dead-code removal.

(2) CACHE BOUND — still enforced. `setCached` is the single insert path, reached only by classifyIntent (the only writer). The removed function was the only other cache writer. I verified empirically: 260 distinct freeform inputs (confirmed 260 distinct cache keys) drive the cache to exactly 200 entries via the eviction branch — the bound holds and the rewritten test genuinely exercises eviction (would report size 260 and fail if the bound regressed). Not false confidence.

(3) REGEX-FIRST + LLM-TIEBREAK ACROSS TIERS — correct and unchanged. agent.service.ts:250-266 gates skipLlmClassification on embedded/constrained tier AND high/medium regex confidence; capable tiers always run the LLM; low-confidence on constrained tiers still falls through to the LLM. d73ed98 only ADDED a comment above the prompt; this logic was not modified.

(4) `source: 'llm'` DEAD UNION MEMBER — cosmetic, not a defect. No path produces it now, but the union being wider than its value space is safe: typecheck passes (tsc --noEmit exit 0), runtime unaffected, and `result.source` is only ever read into a log string (intentClassifier.ts:95).

Verification ran: intent-classifier.test.ts (10 passed), tsc --noEmit (exit 0), eslint on the changed file (exit 0), and empirical probes on cache-key distinctness and eviction. Note: an unrelated untracked file tests/_adv_repro.test.ts exists in the tree from a prior session — not part of this commit and left untouched.

The Q-524 sandbox:true change is sound — the preload only uses contextBridge, process.argv (which carries additionalArguments, the officially documented sandbox-safe channel for the auth token), and process.env (present in Electron's sandboxed-preload polyfill; getVersion degrades via `|| "0.0.1"`), so auth-token delivery survives sandboxing. But the Q-534 tray-icon fix is BROKEN: the icon path is resolved one directory level too shallow, so it points at a file that never exists, and the try/catch guard silently masks the failure — the invisible menu-bar item the commit claims to fix persists in both dev and the packaged asar. Q-534 is effectively unfixed while the commit, journal entry, and code comment all assert it is fixed (false confidence).

defects.description: The tray-icon path is off by one directory level, so the Q-534 fix does not actually load favicon.png and the tray stays invisible — the exact bug the commit claims to fix. main.ts is at src/electron/main.ts and, with tsconfig outDir:./dist + rootDir:./ (and package.json main = dist/src/electron/main.js), compiles to dist/src/electron/main.js. Thus __dirname = <app>/dist/src/electron. path.join(__dirname,'..','..','public','favicon.png') resolves to <app>/dist/public/favicon.png, which does not exist (verified: fs.existsSync = false). The bundled asset is at app-root <app>/public/favicon.png (public/** in electron-builder files:), which needs THREE '..' from dist/src/electron. The code comment ('dist/electron -> ../../public') and commit message mis-model main.js as living at dist/electron (two levels) rather than dist/src/electron (three levels). The pattern was copied from src/app.ts:89 which is CORRECT there only because app.ts sits one level shallower (dist/src, so ../../public = app-root/public). Inside the packaged asar the layout is identical (asar/dist/src/electron + asar/public), so ../../public is wrong there too — the commit's 'holds in dev and inside the packaged asar' claim is false on both counts.
defects.failureScenario: Launch the app (electron . in dev, or the packaged .app/.dmg). createFromPath('<app>/dist/public/favicon.png') is called on a nonexistent file; per Electron docs it returns an empty NativeImage rather than throwing. So the try/catch catches nothing, loaded.isEmpty() is true, the `if (!loaded.isEmpty())` guard is false, and icon stays nativeImage.createEmpty(). new Tray(createEmpty()) renders an invisible menu-bar item — identical to the pre-fix Q-534 behavior. No error, no log; developer sees 'typecheck:src clean' and believes it fixed. Fix: use path.join(__dirname,'..','..','..','public','favicon.png').

bugs.description: downloadedModelsSection's .onDelete deletes by index into `installedModels` while `deleteModel(_:)` mutates that same array mid-loop (it calls reloadModelStorage(), which reassigns installedModels). `offsets.forEach { deleteModel(installedModels[$0]) }` re-reads the shrinking array on every iteration, so multi-row deletes read stale/out-of-range indices. The codebase's own ConversationHistoryView.swift (lines 132-137) documents exactly this hazard and snapshots stable ids BEFORE mutating — SettingsView does not.
bugs.failureScenario: User enters edit mode on Settings > Storage > Downloaded models with 3+ installed models and selects two rows to delete (IndexSet e.g. {1,2}). Iteration 1 deletes installedModels[1] and reloadModelStorage() shrinks the array to 2 elements (valid indices 0,1). Iteration 2 evaluates installedModels[2] on the now-2-element array → Swift array index out of range → crash. With {0,1} it doesn't crash but deletes the wrong model (index 1 after the shift points at what was originally index 2).

bugs.description: In download(_:), when DownloadCoordinator.claim(filename) fails (the target file is already being written by another downloader, e.g. the onboarding installer per Q-003), the task sets states[entry.id] = .downloading(0) and clears tasks[entry.id] = nil, then returns — with no task and no code path that will ever advance that state. Meanwhile downloadAllMissing() (line 552) polls `while case .downloading = self.state(of: entry) { try? await Task.sleep(300ms) }`. Because the state is permanently stuck at .downloading(0), that loop never exits.
bugs.failureScenario: User taps 'Download the complete library' while a model in the queue (same filename) is already being fetched by the onboarding installer. downloadAllMissing calls download(entry); the spawned task's claim() returns false, sets state to .downloading(0) with tasks[entry.id]=nil, and returns. The poll loop for that entry spins forever (busy-waits every 300ms), permanently blocking the sequential queue so none of the remaining models in the 'download all' batch ever start, and the rail badge shows a stuck download that no longer has a task backing it.

bugs.description: AgentSession.onChange writes Compose state (steps/answer/running/haltReason) directly, with no marshaling onto the main dispatcher, while session.run(g) is launched on Dispatchers.IO (line 195). AgentSession fires onChange() synchronously on the calling thread (verified: quenderin-core/AgentSession.kt lines 72/76/84/108 call onChange() with no dispatch), so every emit during a run mutates Compose snapshot state from the IO thread. This is the exact twin of the Q-228 bug ChatScreen fixed by marshaling chat.onChange onto Dispatchers.Main.immediate (ChatScreen.kt lines 118-124); AgentScreen never got the fix.
bugs.failureScenario: User taps Run. session.run() executes on Dispatchers.IO; each planning/tool step invokes onChange() which sets `steps = this.steps`, `running = this.isRunning`, etc. from the IO thread. Off-main-thread writes to Compose MutableState violate the snapshot threading model — recompositions can be missed, torn, or (in strict/debug builds) throw, so agent steps and the final answer can fail to render or the screen can crash intermittently under real streaming.

bugs.description: The auto-scroll LaunchedEffect keys on (messages.size, busy). During a streaming reply the assistant message is replaced token-by-token via onChange (messages = next) but the list SIZE never changes — only the last element's text grows. So the effect does not re-fire while a long reply streams in, and the growing bubble scrolls off the bottom of the viewport instead of following the newest text. It should key on the transcript's content/length (e.g. the last message's length), not its element count.
bugs.failureScenario: User sends a prompt that produces a multi-paragraph reply. As tokens stream, messages.size stays constant, so animateScrollToItem is never re-invoked; the visible bottom of the reply falls below the fold and the user has to scroll manually to watch it generate.

bugs.description: Off-by-one in the auto-scroll target. The LazyColumn always renders a DayDivider as item index 0 (line 164), then the messages, then an optional TypingBubble. So the last valid index is (messages.size + (busy?1:0)) because of the +1 divider. But the effect computes count = messages.size + (busy?1:0) and scrolls to count-1, which is one row short of the last item — the DayDivider is not counted. The newest message / typing bubble is therefore never scrolled fully into view.
bugs.failureScenario: New message arrives while the transcript is longer than the viewport: animateScrollToItem lands on the second-to-last row, leaving the newest bubble (or the typing indicator) partially or fully below the fold on every send.

bugs.description: Model delete runs blocking filesystem I/O on the main thread. The Delete button's onClick calls ModelManager(FileModelStorage(modelsDir), ...).delete(installed.id) followed by reloadModels() (directory enumeration + summing file sizes) directly in the onClick lambda, which executes on the main thread. Deleting a multi-GB GGUF file and then re-listing the models dir synchronously blocks the UI thread. reloadModels() is also invoked from LaunchedEffect(model.id) (line 77), which runs on the Main (AndroidUiDispatcher) dispatcher, so that disk enumeration is on the main thread too.
bugs.failureScenario: User deletes a large downloaded model on Settings. delete() unlinks a several-GB file and reloadModels() re-enumerates the directory, all on the main thread — the UI freezes for the duration and can trip an ANR on slower storage.

bugs.description: In generate(), GetFieldID("cancelRequested") and GetMethodID("recommendedThreads") (lines 112-113) can return null while leaving a pending NoSuchFieldError/NoSuchMethodError, and this pending exception is never checked or cleared before the decode loop runs. Unlike thermalPoll (line 143) and emit (line 126), the RESOLUTION step here has no ExceptionCheck. Once an exception is pending, the very next JNI call in the token loop (emit -> make_jstring -> NewByteArray, or the cancelled()/GetBooleanField upcall) is undefined behavior and ART aborts the whole process.
bugs.failureScenario: On a release build, R8/ProGuard renames or removes LlamaEngine.cancelRequested / recommendedThreads() (or the Kotlin signature drifts from "Z"/"()I"). GetFieldID/GetMethodID return null with a pending exception that is never cleared; generation then proceeds and the first JNI call inside generateWithKVReuse (a JNI call with a pending exception) causes ART to abort the process instead of streaming a reply.

bugs.description: In generate(), GetMethodID(cls, "onToken", ...) at line 100 can return null and leave a pending NoSuchMethodError. The code only guards on `if (!on_token)` (line 118) to decide streaming vs accumulate, but never clears the pending exception. Execution then continues into the thiz block (GetFieldID/GetMethodID at 112-113) and the decode loop with an exception already pending -> subsequent JNI calls are UB.
bugs.failureScenario: If the sink object passed to nativeCompleteChatStreaming/nativeCompleteStreaming does not expose an onToken(String) method with the exact signature (e.g. an interface renamed by R8, or a wrong object passed), GetMethodID returns null with a pending NoSuchMethodError; the code silently treats it as non-streaming and keeps making JNI calls with an uncleared exception, leading to a process abort rather than a clean error.

bugs.description: make_jstring() uses the results of FindClass("java/lang/String") (line 69), GetMethodID for the ([BLjava/lang/String;)V ctor (line 70), and NewStringUTF("UTF-8") (line 71) without any null-check before dereferencing them in NewObject (line 72). If NewStringUTF returns null under OOM (charset), NewObject is invoked with a null charset argument; if FindClass/GetMethodID ever returned null (with a pending exception), GetMethodID on a null class / NewObject on a null methodID is UB. There is also no ExceptionCheck after SetByteArrayRegion.
bugs.failureScenario: Under memory pressure NewStringUTF("UTF-8") returns null; NewObject is then called with a null charset, and although the pending exception usually propagates, the intermediate JNI calls run with no guard. In the worse case a null jmethodID is passed to NewObject, which is undefined behavior rather than a clean OOM surfaced to Kotlin (defeating throw_oom's contract described at line 64).

bugs.description: tokenize() computes `int n = -llama_tokenize(vocab, ..., nullptr, 0, add_bos, true)` (line 53) and allocates `std::vector<llama_token> tokens(n)` (line 54). The code assumes llama_tokenize always returns a non-positive value on the zero-capacity probing call. If it ever returns a positive count (0 tokens fit in the 0-size buffer, or an API/version change), n becomes negative and is passed to the vector constructor as size_t, yielding a gigantic allocation and std::bad_alloc/crash. The sign convention is a hard, unchecked assumption on an external API pinned by comment only.
bugs.failureScenario: A future llama.cpp bump (the file itself notes "the API moves") changes the zero-buffer return convention, or an edge-case input returns 0/positive; -value makes n negative, `std::vector<llama_token> tokens(n)` requests ~SIZE_MAX elements and crashes the process on that generation instead of failing gracefully.
